my_sentence: I eat my lunch.
correction: I ate my lunch.
explanation: (assistant note) The teacher asked whether Jack had eaten lunch, so the completed action needs the simple past form "ate," not the present form "eat."
correct_examples: I ate my lunch before class.
She ate at noon.
context: Small talk about what Jack had done before class.

my_sentence: They can do it in their best.
correction: They can do their best.
explanation: The fixed expression is "do your best," with no "it" or "in." The teacher immediately supplied the complete phrase.
correct_examples: They can do their best to finish the task.
I always do my best at work.
context: Explaining how poor people may focus on one opportunity.

my_sentence: They can invest many projects.
correction: They can invest in many projects.
explanation: Use "invest in" before the thing receiving money. The teacher inserted the missing preposition.
correct_examples: They invest in several small companies.
She invested in a new project.
context: Comparing how rich and poor people invest.

my_sentence: If one of them meet some problem...
correction: If one of them meets some problems...
explanation: (assistant note) The subject is singular "one," so the present-tense verb needs "-s": "meets." The teacher also drilled the plural "problems."
correct_examples: If one of them meets a problem, the team helps.
One of the projects needs more money.
context: What investors do when one project has difficulties.

my_sentence: When they find the project has too much problems...
correction: When they find that the project has too many problems...
explanation: Use "many" with the plural countable noun "problems," not "much." The teacher explicitly corrected the quantifier and plural form.
correct_examples: The project has too many problems.
We had too many tasks to finish.
context: Deciding when to stop investing in a project.

my_sentence: They will draw back their money.
correction: They will draw their money back.
explanation: With the separable phrasal verb "draw back," the object can go between the verb and particle. The teacher moved "their money" into that position.
correct_examples: They decided to draw their money back.
The investor pulled his money out.
context: Withdrawing money from a troubled project.

my_sentence: I find that don't have enough money, it's the biggest burden in my life.
correction: Not having enough money is the biggest burden in my life.
explanation: A gerund phrase can act as the subject of the sentence: "Not having enough money..." The teacher replaced the incomplete "I find that don't have" structure with that phrase.
correct_examples: Not having enough time is stressful.
Having savings makes emergencies easier.
context: Describing Jack's biggest burden.

my_sentence: I will don't have enough money to recover.
correction: I won't have enough money for my medical bills.
explanation: Do not combine "will" with "don't"; the negative future is "will not" or "won't." The teacher also replaced "to recover" with the intended expense, "for my medical bills."
correct_examples: I won't have enough money for treatment.
He won't have enough savings to pay the bill.
context: Worrying about the cost of a serious illness.

my_sentence: I will not pay education fee for my daughter.
correction: I will not be able to pay my daughter's educational fees.
explanation: (assistant note) Use "will not be able to" to express a future lack of ability. The teacher drilled that verb group; "my daughter's educational fees" clarifies whose fees Jack meant.
correct_examples: I may not be able to pay the school fees.
They will be able to cover their child's tuition.
context: Worrying about paying for his daughter's education.

my_sentence: Some more diseases will become burden for me.
correction: Some more diseases will become a burden for me.
explanation: The singular countable noun "burden" needs the article "a." The teacher inserted it immediately.
correct_examples: Medical costs can become a burden.
Debt was a heavy burden for the family.
context: Other burdens Jack may face in the future.

my_sentence: Our organ can't be recover.
correction: Our organs can't be recovered.
explanation: A passive after a modal uses "modal + be + past participle": "can't be recovered." The teacher also prompted the plural "organs."
correct_examples: Some organs can't be fully recovered.
The damaged tissue can be treated.
context: Why some serious diseases cannot be cured completely.

my_sentence: That's no problem.
correction: That's not a problem.
explanation: Use "not a problem" to negate the complete noun phrase. The teacher recast the sentence with "not" and the article "a."
correct_examples: That's not a problem for me.
Eating there is not a problem if the food is safe.
context: Whether eating raw fish is acceptable.

my_sentence: They need to provide safety and healthy food.
correction: They need to provide safe and healthy food.
explanation: The two coordinated words should both be adjectives modifying "food": "safe and healthy." The teacher supplied "safe" instead of the noun "safety."
correct_examples: The restaurant serves safe and healthy food.
Make sure the raw fish is safe to eat.
context: Choosing a suitable restaurant for raw fish.

my_sentence: You will shake.
correction: You will get sick.
explanation: "Shake" describes a physical movement, but Jack meant becoming ill after eating unsafe fish. The teacher supplied the natural phrase "get sick."
correct_examples: You could get sick from unsafe food.
He got sick after eating raw seafood.
context: The risk of eating contaminated raw fish.

my_sentence: You will be sent to hospital for many days.
correction: You will be sent to the hospital for many days.
explanation: In the variety of English used in this lesson, the fixed expression is "be sent to the hospital." The teacher inserted "the" and had Jack repeat the sentence.
correct_examples: He was sent to the hospital.
They took her to the hospital immediately.
context: The possible result of food poisoning.

my_sentence: They do not get boiled, but they get proceeded by other methods to kill these disease.
correction: They are not boiled, but they are processed using other methods to kill these pathogens.
explanation: (assistant note) Use a passive form for what is done to the food: "are processed," not "get proceeded." The teacher repeatedly modeled the passive "they are filtered"; "pathogens" expresses the intended disease-causing organisms.
correct_examples: The fish is processed before it is served.
The food is treated using cold temperatures.
context: Explaining how raw fish can be made safer without boiling it.

my_sentence: If you go to some Western restaurant...
correction: If you go to some Western restaurants...
explanation: After "some," a countable noun normally takes the plural form. The teacher repeated "restaurants" twice.
correct_examples: Some Western restaurants serve rare steak.
We visited several Italian restaurants.
context: Ordering steak with different levels of doneness.

my_sentence: In China, I rarely see some restaurants provide raw beef.
correction: In China, I rarely see restaurants providing raw beef.
explanation: (assistant note) The transcript's "really" is restored as "rarely" from the negative context. After "see" here, the teacher prompted the "-ing" form "providing," and the bare plural "restaurants" fits a general observation.
correct_examples: I rarely see restaurants providing raw beef.
We often see shops selling fresh fruit.
context: Whether restaurants in China serve raw beef.

my_sentence: For me, I more prefer Asian restaurants.
correction: I prefer Asian cuisine more.
explanation: Place "more" after the object, not before "prefer." The teacher also replaced "restaurants" with "cuisine" because the question was about kinds of food.
correct_examples: I prefer Asian cuisine more.
She prefers home cooking to fast food.
context: Choosing between Asian and Western cuisine.

my_sentence: It's very popular in the world.
correction: It's very popular around the world.
explanation: The idiomatic phrase for worldwide popularity is "around the world." The teacher explicitly supplied it and asked Jack to repeat it.
correct_examples: Fast food is popular around the world.
The song became famous around the world.
context: The global popularity of Western fast food.

my_sentence: I think it's a plausible.
correction: I think it's plausible.
explanation: (assistant note) "Plausible" is an adjective, so it cannot take the article "a" unless a noun follows it.
correct_examples: I think the idea is plausible.
That sounds like a plausible explanation.
context: Evaluating Person B's view of poverty.

my_sentence: I think in my opinion, I don't think that's so totally true.
correction: In my opinion, I don't think it's totally true.
explanation: "I think" and "in my opinion" repeat the same stance, so use only one. The teacher removed the repetition and recast "that's so totally true" as "it's totally true."
correct_examples: In my opinion, it isn't completely true.
I don't think that claim is totally accurate.
context: Disagreeing partly with Person B's argument.

my_sentence: If she stay at school... / She usually sleep from three o'clock to five o'clock.
correction: If she stays at school... / She usually sleeps from three o'clock to five o'clock.
explanation: (assistant note) With the singular third-person subject "she" in the simple present, the verbs need "-s": "stays" and "sleeps." The teacher modeled "She stays..." while clarifying the situation.
correct_examples: If she stays at home, she sleeps later.
She usually sleeps in the afternoon.
context: Describing his daughter's afternoon nap schedule.

my_sentence: Stay at all night.
correction: Stay out all night.
explanation: Use the phrasal verb "stay out" for remaining away from home late. The teacher immediately recast the phrase with "out."
correct_examples: Some people stay out all night at bars.
I rarely stay out late.
context: Examples of frivolous activities.

my_sentence: I bought some smartphones and laptops that are not that well.
correction: I bought some smartphones and laptops that were unneeded.
explanation: The relative clause describes items bought in the past, so use "were." The teacher supplied both "that were" and the intended adjective "unneeded."
correct_examples: I bought several devices that were unneeded.
He returned the accessories that were defective.
context: Expensive items Jack later considered unnecessary.

my_sentence: I spend a lot of my time to search these things online and buy them.
correction: I spent a lot of time searching for these things online and buying them.
explanation: (assistant note) The story is about an earlier period, so use "spent." After "spend time," use an "-ing" form: "searching" and "buying," not an infinitive.
correct_examples: I spent hours searching for new devices.
She spends too much time browsing online shops.
context: How shopping for gadgets wasted time and money.

my_sentence: Every year I will buy new one instead of the old one.
correction: I wanted to buy a new one instead of the old one every year.
explanation: The sentence describes a repeated desire in the past, so use a past form rather than future "will." The teacher modeled "I wanted to buy a new one instead of the old one."
correct_examples: Every year, I wanted to buy a new phone.
I used to replace my laptop too often.
context: Buying the latest smart devices when he was younger.

my_sentence: I find it's not necessary.
correction: I found that it wasn't necessary.
explanation: The realization happened in the past, so both verbs should be in the past: "found" and "wasn't." The teacher drilled the past form "found."
correct_examples: I found that the upgrade wasn't necessary.
Later, I realized that I didn't need a new phone.
context: Realizing that yearly device upgrades were unnecessary.

my_sentence: It's not worth to change your smart devices.
correction: It's not worth changing your smart devices.
explanation: (assistant note) After "worth," use a gerund: "worth changing," not an infinitive with "to."
correct_examples: It isn't worth changing phones every year.
The small upgrade isn't worth paying for.
context: Whether small new features justify replacing devices.

my_sentence: I was attacked by the new features.
correction: I was attracted by the new features.
explanation: "Attacked" means harmed, while "attracted" means interested or drawn toward something. The teacher immediately supplied and repeated "attracted."
correct_examples: I was attracted by the new features.
The bright design attracted many customers.
context: Why new devices appealed to Jack.

my_sentence: If he or she is failed, he or she will lose everything.
correction: If he or she fails, he or she can lose everything.
explanation: (assistant note) "Fail" is normally active here, so use "fails," not the passive "is failed." For a possible result rather than a certainty, "can lose" fits the teacher's later recast.
correct_examples: If the business fails, the owner can lose everything.
If she fails, she can start again.
context: The financial risk of starting a business with little capital.

my_sentence: He or she had to begin again.
correction: He or she has to start from square one.
explanation: (assistant note) For this general present situation, use "has to" rather than "had to." The teacher supplied the idiom "start from square one" for beginning again after losing all previous progress.
correct_examples: After the business failed, she had to start from square one.
We lost the files and started from square one.
context: Recovering after a failed business.

my_sentence: Many years ago, the company is so small. Most of them sold their shares and only get a little money.
correction: Many years ago, the company was very small. Most of them sold their shares and only got a little money.
explanation: (assistant note) "Many years ago" establishes a completed past period, so keep the states and actions in the simple past: "was, sold, got."
correct_examples: Years ago, the company was small and its shares were cheap.
They sold early and got only a little money.
context: Early employees selling shares before companies became very large.

my_sentence: They will become billionaire.
correction: They will become billionaires.
explanation: The plural subject "they" refers to several people, so the countable complement must also be plural. The teacher explicitly supplied "billionaires."
correct_examples: Some early investors became billionaires.
They hope to become successful business owners.
context: What early shareholders could gain if a company succeeds.

my_sentence: For some poor people, they lack of capital and self-confidence.
correction: Some poor people lack capital and self-confidence.
explanation: When "lack" is a verb, it takes a direct object with no "of": "lack capital." The teacher later modeled the correct structure, "when you lack capital."
correct_examples: Some small businesses lack capital.
When you lack capital, growth can be difficult.
context: Explaining the disadvantages of being poor.
